findings.description: _is_expired returns age < ttl, which is inverted: it reports fresh entries as expired and expired entries as still valid.
Due to the inverted _is_expired, get() deletes fresh L1 entries and returns stale ones as cache hits.
pickle.loads on data read from Redis is a remote-code-execution vector if an attacker can write to the Redis store.
pickle.dumps serializes arbitrary objects into Redis, enabling the unsafe deserialization path and unbounded/untyped payloads.
The _local L1 dict has no size bound or eviction policy, so it grows unbounded and can exhaust memory.
pickle.loads has no error handling; corrupt or incompatible bytes from Redis raise and crash get() instead of treating it as a miss.
RedisClient.setex ignores ttl_seconds, so the L2 tier never enforces TTL expiry on stored values.
get_or_compute treats None as 'not cached', so a legitimately-None computed value is never cached and is recomputed on every call.
get() returns None for both a cache miss and a stored None value, making the two states indistinguishable to callers.
No synchronization around _local and stats; concurrent access from multiple threads races on reads, writes, and counter increments.
Loading from Redis stamps the L1 entry with the current time, resetting the TTL clock relative to the original L2 write so a near-expired value gets a full fresh local lifetime.
An L1 miss that is served from L2 (Redis) is counted only as a hit; the L1 miss is never recorded, skewing per-tier hit/miss metrics.
CacheStats.local_entries is only updated in set(); the get()/Redis-population path leaves it stale, and export_stats bypasses it via len(self._local), making the field inconsistent and unused.
CacheStats.local_entries duplicates information already derivable from len(self._local) and is maintained inconsistently, suggesting it should be a computed property.
